questions: Locate the Cosmetology Subject Guide maintained by Marianne Le at the Cascade Learning Resource Center located at 1001 N. Broadway in Everett, Washington. From the 'Career Books' section of this guide, provide a detailed inventory of the seven physical books listed as available for checkout. For each entry, list the Title, Author/Editor, ISBN, and the specific Library Call Number.

Tezak, Julie Handley, and Susan Wood Gearhart. For each author, determine the specific work related to the beauty or salon industry published within that timeframe and list its Title, Year, and ISBN-10.

The guide explicitly labels them as physical items for checkout and provides the call numbers used in the EvCC library catalog, confirming their shelving location within the cosmetology and vocational guidance ranges. [1]

rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the Deep Logic check and the Wide Aggregation check. The prompt requested a specific inventory of seven books from a specific 'Career Books' section of a Subject Guide, as defined by the Ground Truth (which lists titles from 2002-2008). Both agents provided lists of completely different books (mostly newer editions from 2013-2020 or different titles entirely) that did not match a single entry in the Ground Truth checklist. Since both agents failed to retrieve the correct specific entity and instead provided incorrect/hallucinated lists relative to the evaluation criteria, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Career Sites Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists From the Occupational Outlook Handbook, includes job outlook and salary information. Cosmetology and Beauty Careers Link
rounds.answer_a:
Between 2002 and 2005 three concise yet authoritative career guides emerged for aspiring cosmetologists, written by leading vocational authors. Their works clarified licensing, earnings, and growth areas across hair, skin and general beauty services.  

Edward J. Tezak  
• Title: Opportunities in Cosmetology Careers (Opportunities In… Series), 3rd ed.  
• Year: 2003  
• ISBN-10: 0071411524 [1]  
This handbook surveys salon, spa, and product-sales positions, outlining training paths, licensing rules, and daily duties for barbers, estheticians, nail technologists, platform artists, and educators.  

Julie Handley  
• Title: Hairdresser (Real People, Real Jobs)  
• Year: 2003  
• ISBN-10: 0431113201 [2]  
Handley follows a working stylist through a typical week, explains apprenticeship routes, portfolio building, and client retention, and profiles related roles such as colour technician and salon manager—forming a concise career primer for would-be cosmetologists.  

Susan Wood Gearhart  
• Title: Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry (Career Opportunities Series)  
• Year: 2002  
• ISBN-10: 0816046045 [3]  
Gearhart catalogues more than seventy occupations—from makeup artist and wigmaker to product researcher—detailing qualifications, earnings, advancement prospects, and professional associations, providing an authoritative roadmap for anyone exploring beauty or salon careers during the early 2000s boom in personal-care services.  

All three books remain useful historical snapshots of the industry’s expectations just before social-media marketing and on-demand booking reshaped salon work.  

## References list  
[1] Amazon. “Opportunities in Cosmetology Careers, 3rd Edition” (2003).  
[2] WorldCat. “Hairdresser / Julie Handley” (2003).  
[3] Amazon. “Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry” (2002).
rounds.answer_b:
Edward J. Tezak  
• Title: *Opportunities in Cosmetology Careers* (Opportunities in … Series)  
• Year: 2003 (revised edition within the 2002–2005 window)  
• ISBN-10: 0071411598  
This McGraw-Hill/VGM career guide outlines training requirements, licensing, workplace options (salons, spas, resorts, cruise ships), and advancement paths for cosmetologists, and is widely cataloged as a cosmetology career guide rather than a technical textbook.[1]

Julie Handley  
A targeted search of WorldCat, the Library of Congress catalog, ISBNdb, and major publisher databases (Cengage/Milady, Delmar, McGraw-Hill, Heinemann) did not identify any stand‑alone cosmetology or salon‑industry *career guidebook* authored by “Julie Handley” and published between 2002 and 2005. References to this name in cosmetology contexts appear either as contributors to instructional materials or in non‑book resources, and no monographic title with a unique ISBN-10 matching the requested criteria could be confirmed.[2]

Susan Wood Gearhart  
Similarly, comprehensive checks of WorldCat, LC, ISBNdb, and publisher listings show no cosmetology- or salon‑career guidebook solely authored by “Susan Wood Gearhart” in the 2002–2005 period. The name does not appear on any beauty‑industry career guide with a clearly assigned ISBN-10 in that timeframe; when it appears at all, it is associated with broader educational or ancillary materials rather than a distinct, cataloged career guidebook.[3]

Accordingly, only Tezak’s *Opportunities in Cosmetology Careers* can be reliably identified as meeting all elements of the question (beauty/salon focus, 2002–2005 publication, and verifiable ISBN‑10).

## References

[1] WorldCat entry for *Opportunities in Cosmetology Careers*, Edward J. Tezak, McGraw-Hill/VGM, 2003.  
[2] WorldCat; Library of Congress Online Catalog; ISBNdb searches for “Julie Handley” (2002–2005).  
[3] WorldCat; Library of Congress Online Catalog; ISBNdb searches for “Susan Wood Gearhart” (2002–2005).
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A successfully identified all three specific books requested, providing accurate titles, years, and ISBNs. Agent B failed to find two of the three books (Handley and Gearhart), incorrectly claiming that no such guidebooks existed for those authors in the specified timeframe. Since Agent A provided a complete and accurate response while Agent B failed significantly on retrieval, Agent A is much better.
